Hi Sarah, good to see you. How is your week going so far?
Busy. Same as always.
I can imagine. It is the end of the quarter for you too, isn't it? Mine started badly: my flight was three hours late.
Three hours? That is painful. Where were you flying from?
Sao Paulo. Four hours at the gate, and I read a whole book. By the way, did you have a good weekend?
I did, actually. I played tennis on Saturday. First time in two years.
Oh really? I did not know you played. My daughter started last year, and now she beats me, which is not good for my ego.
That happens fast. Wait until she is fifteen.
I am not ready for that. Anyway, we have a full agenda today. Shall we start?
Busy, I can imagine. Mine started badly.
Did you have a good weekend, by the way?
Oh really? I did not know you played tennis.
Anyway, we have a full agenda -- shall we start?

Diogo, a word before your call with Chicago. I know you think small talk is a waste of time. In the United States it is not: it is how people decide if they trust you. Two minutes, no more, but you have to do it. Ask an open question -- how is your week going, how was the weekend. Then react to what she says, and add something about yourself, or it becomes an interview. Safe topics: weather, weekend, travel, sport, food. Dangerous topics: politics, salary, religion, and the age of anybody. And when it is time, close it yourself. Say anyway, and start the meeting. If you do not close it, the small talk eats your agenda.

Hi Sarah, good to see you. How is your week going so far? ... Busy, I can imagine -- it is the end of the quarter for you too, isn't it? Mine started badly: my flight from Sao Paulo was three hours late, so I had a lot of time to read at the airport. Did you have a good weekend, by the way? ... Oh really? I did not know you played tennis. My daughter started last year, and now she wins against me, which is not good for my ego. Chicago must be cold for tennis in June, no? ... Right. Anyway, we have a full agenda today, so shall we start? I will share my screen, and I would like to leave ten minutes at the end for your questions.

Diogo opens the call and asks how Sarah's week is going. She answers in three words, and he adds something about himself: his flight was three hours late. Then he asks about her weekend, and she says she played tennis. He reacts, and tells her that his daughter now beats him. Finally, he closes the small talk and starts the meeting.
